Jamshedji Tata

# Jamshedji Tata

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Mar 02, 2021

Share

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!zTC4!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F2ebaac78-a545-4e94-8893-9cf2ba5b4f54_184x179.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!zTC4!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F2ebaac78-a545-4e94-8893-9cf2ba5b4f54_184x179.jpeg)

Jamshedji Nusserwanji Tata is one of those figures who almost feels like a bridge between eras, rooted in the traditions of 19th‑century India, yet dreaming ahead into the industrial future. He founded Tata Steel, India’s first integrated steel plant, which became the backbone of modern infrastructure. He also laid the foundation for the Indian Institute of Science (IISc), nurturing scientific research long before it was fashionable.

Jamshedpur, the city named after him, was envisioned as a model industrial township, clean, organized, and humane in its design. What sets him apart is not just the industries he built, but the foresight with which he imagined India’s self‑reliance. He believed in blending philanthropy with enterprise, ensuring that progress was not just profit‑driven but people‑centered.

**Early Life**

He was born on 3 March 1839 in Navsari, southern Gujarat, to Nusserwanji Ratanji Tata and Jeevanbai Nusserwanji Tata. The family belonged to the priestly class, respected but financially constrained. His mother tongue was Gujarati, and he grew up in a household steeped in tradition.

His parents noticed his talent for mental calculation early, which convinced them he needed a modern education. At 14, he joined his father in Bombay, a city that was rapidly becoming India’s commercial hub. He enrolled at **Elphinstone College**, where he completed his studies as a *“Green Scholar”*, the equivalent of a graduate. While still a student, he married **Hirabai Daboo**, balancing personal responsibilities with academic pursuits.

After completing his studies at Elphinstone College, he joined his father’s export-trading firm in Bombay. He helped establish strong branches in **Japan, China, Europe, and the United States**, showing remarkable foresight for international trade at a time when few Indian firms thought globally.

His father sent him to China to learn the intricacies of business there, including the opium trade, which was a major commodity at the time. While traveling through China, Jamsetji observed the **booming cotton industry**. Rather than focusing on opium, he recognized cotton as a sector with immense potential for profit and long-term growth.

**Business**

He stayed in his father’s company until age 29, gaining deep experience in trade and global markets. With ₹21,000 capital, he launched his own trading company, rather audacious move considering his priestly background.

He purchased a bankrupt oil mill at Chinchpokli, converted it into a cotton mill, and named it **Alexandra Mill**, and sold it at a profit within 2 years. He established the **Central India Spinning, Weaving, and Manufacturing Company** in Nagpur, a decison many saw as rather strange, as Bombay then was the cotton manufacturing hub. But Jamsetji saw strategic advantages, proximity to cotton-growing regions, cheaper land, and better logistics.

He had four goals in life, India’s own steel industry, a foundation for modern infrastructure and independence. An advanced research university to nurture scientific talent in India.A hotel that would rival the best in the world, a symbol of Indian pride and hospitality and a Hydroelectric plant.

During his lifetime though, J.N.Tata would only be able see the dream of a world class hotel come true, Taj Mahal in Mumbai. The Taj opened in December 1903, was the first building in Bombay to be lit by electricity.

Its imported chandeliers, German elevators, and English butlers symbolized cosmopolitan luxury. Though Sitaram Vaidya and D.N. Mirza were the Indian architects, the final design was refined by W.A. Chambers, giving it that Indo-Saracenic style with domes and arches. It also functioned as a 600-bed hospital during the war.

**Steel Plant**

However the dream of the Steel Plant, was something closest to his heart, in Manchester, he had attended a lecture by Thomas Carlyle, who spoke of the dignity of labor and the transformative power of industry. That moment planted the seed of steel in Tata’s imagination.

Seeing Britain’s industrial might firsthand, he realized India needed its own backbone of steel to stand tall. The quote attributed to Carlyle, *“Control iron, acquire gold”* (sometimes paraphrased as *“The nation that controls iron controls gold too”*), was said to have struck Jamsetji Tata deeply during his time in Manchester.

Tata’s steel dream faced daunting obstacles. India hadn’t yet experienced the Industrial Revolution in full, so the infrastructure, expertise, and capital were scarce. But Tata’s determination was fueled by scientific reports and his own conviction that India must industrialize. The German geologist Ritter von Schwartz surveyed iron ore deposits in Chanda district (present-day Chandrapur, Maharashtra). His findings gave Tata tangible proof that India had the raw materials to support a steel industry.

He meticulously studied steelmaking processes across the world, from Bessemer in Britain to open-hearth methods in the U.S. He wanted India’s plant to be world-class, not second-rate. Tata traveled widely, examining steel plants in Europe and America, learning about smelting techniques, furnace designs, and industrial organization.

Perrin, one of the leading metallurgical engineers in the U.S., agreed to come to India to help Tata realize his vision. His expertise gave credibility to the project and reassured skeptics that India could indeed build a modern steel plant.

In Birmingham, Alabama, Tata studied coking processes, critical for converting coal into coke, the fuel that drives blast furnaces. He wanted to understand not just the science but the economics of steel production. At Cleveland, Ohio, he examined the ore market, learning how supply chains, pricing, and industrial logistics worked in a mature steel economy.

Back in India, he faced a stubborn bureaucracy and outright scorn from senior British officials. Many dismissed the idea of an Indian steel plant as “impossible” or “premature,” reflecting colonial prejudice against Indian enterprise. Instead of discouraging him, this opposition sharpened his resolve.

He believed that India’s dignity depended on mastering steel, and he was willing to endure ridicule to pursue it. Instead of discouraging him, this opposition sharpened his resolve. He believed that India’s dignity depended on mastering steel, and he was willing to endure ridicule to pursue it.

*“I will eat every pound of steel the Tatas make.”*

Sir Frederic Upcott’s words epitomized the skepticism and condescension Tata faced from British officials. They couldn’t imagine Indians mastering steel.

In a letter to his son Dorab, Tata outlined his dream of a self-sufficient community for employees, housing, schools, hospitals, and gardens. This wasn’t just industry; it was society-building.

That vision materialized into one of India’s earliest planned cities. Jamshedpur became a model of industrial townships, blending work and life, and symbolizing Tata’s belief that industry must serve humanity.

While he was a philanthropist, he rejected the idea of mere charity or doles. He believed that **education was the true path to empowerment**. He established JN Tata Endowment in 1892 to support meritorious Indian students in pursuing higher studies abroad, open to all irrespective of caste, creed, or class.

Tata believed that by nurturing talent, India could build its own intellectual and industrial strength. He wanted to create leaders, scientists, and professionals who would shape the nation’s future.

**IISC**

Jamsetji Tata’s vision for the **Indian Institute of Science (IISc)** is another testament to his belief that education and research were the true engines of national progress. In the late 19th century, Tata conceived of a world-class scientific institution in India that would rival the best in Europe.

He had met Swami Vivekananda around 1899, who strongly supported it, saying India needed such a center to harness science for progress. He pledged ₹30 lakhs of his own property, to establish the institute. This wasn’t just philanthropy, it was a strategic investment in India’s intellectual future. Tata sought assistance from the government, scientists, and leaders of society, emphasizing that such an institution would benefit the entire nation.

Though he passed away before IISc was established, his vision was carried forward. With support from the Mysore royal family and the British government, IISc was founded in Bangalore in 1909.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!pv43!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F409ed597-ccd2-4904-8198-b91b2d42abd4_1280x858.jpeg)

![undefined](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!pv43!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F409ed597-ccd2-4904-8198-b91b2d42abd4_1280x858.jpeg "undefined")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

While on a business trip to Germany in 1900, he became seriously ill. He sought treatment at Bad Nauheim, a spa town known for its therapeutic waters, and died there on **19 May 1904**, never seeing the steel plant or the Indian Institute of Science come to fruition in his lifetime. He was laid to rest in the **Parsi burial ground at Brookwood Cemetery, Woking, England**, far from the land whose destiny he had worked so tirelessly to shape.

Though he didn’t live to witness his dreams realized, his groundwork ensured they would be carried forward. Within a few years, Tata Steel was founded in Jamshedpur (1907), and IISc opened in Bangalore (1909).
